preparation_method: seasoned with soy sauce and pepper

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of fried egg with minced pork weighs approximately 180 g, representing a typical single-serve portion as sold at Malaysian and Singaporean hawker stalls and coffee shops.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Egg, whole, cooked, fried (FDC ID 173424) and Pork, ground, 84% lean / 16% fat, cooked, pan-broiled (FDC ID 168351); composite estimate weighted approximately 65% egg and 35% minced pork by cooked weight, adjusted for soy sauce seasoning contribution to sodium.
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for fried egg with minced pork as a composite Malaysian dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central using a weighted composite of fried whole egg (FDC 173424) and cooked ground pork 84% lean (FDC 168351) at an approximate 65:35 ratio by cooked weight, with sodium adjusted upward to reflect soy sauce seasoning. Input per_100g values (energy 194 kcal, protein 12.2 g, fat 15.6 g, carbohydrate 1.7 g, sodium 361 mg) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Cholesterol 222 mg/100g is consistent with USDA fried egg data (~294 mg/100g egg × 0.65 ratio plus pork contribution). Saturated fat 5.0 g/100g is plausible. Trans fat 0.06 g/100g is within expected range for pan-fried pork and egg. Calcium 44 mg/100g and iron 1.39 mg/100g are consistent with USDA composite. Vitamin C 0.56 mg/100g is trace, consistent with negligible vegetable content.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic index not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Fried egg with minced pork contains only 1.67 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable for this food as carbohydrate content is negligible and insufficient to elicit a meaningful glycemic response. Per Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to 0 (numeric sentinel for not-applicable foods in this template), gi_category to 'not_applicable', and GL per serving to 0.0. University of Sydney GI Database consulted; no GI entry exists for this food type as it is a negligible-carbohydrate dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 361 mg/100g from soy sauce seasoning, with low dietary fibre (0.28 g/100g) and limited potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 90 g (half plate), request reduced or low-sodium soy sauce, and pair with high-potassium vegetables such as spinach or broccoli to partially offset the sodium load; saturated fat at 5.0 g/100g also warrants moderation to protect arterial health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 1.67 g/100g and sugar 0.56 g/100g are negligible; high protein 12.22 g/100g supports glycaemic modulation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried egg with minced pork has negligible carbohydrate content and poses minimal glycaemic impact for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, portion size should be moderated to limit cholesterol intake (222 mg/100g), and the dish should be paired with non-starchy vegetables and a low-GI carbohydrate source for a balanced meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 222 mg/100g, saturated fat 5.0 g/100g, and sodium 361 mg/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish sparingly; a single 180 g serving delivers approximately 400 mg cholesterol and 9 g saturated fat, approaching or exceeding daily limits, and the sodium content of 650 mg per serving further burdens cardiovascular health — substituting minced chicken or tofu for pork and using low-sodium soy sauce are recommended modifications.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Minced pork (red meat) is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6), amplifying pro-inflammatory pathways; saturated fat at 5.0 g/100g is at the RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise this dish due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of minced pork; substituting minced chicken breast or firm tofu significantly reduces the inflammatory load while preserving the dish's protein contribution.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Egg with Minced Pork (肉碎煎蛋, Rou Sui Jian Dan) is a classic Malaysian and Singaporean hawker and coffee-shop dish consisting of whole eggs beaten or layered with seasoned minced pork, then pan-fried in oil until the edges are golden and crispy while the interior remains tender and savoury. The minced pork is typically seasoned with light soy sauce, white or black pepper, and sometimes a dash of sesame oil before being combined with the egg mixture. The result is a protein-rich, flavourful dish with a slightly crispy exterior, soft egg interior, and savoury umami depth from the soy-seasoned pork. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 194 kcal, 12.2 g protein, 15.6 g fat, and only 1.7 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it a low-carbohydrate, high-protein option. Cholesterol is elevated at 222 mg/100g and sodium at 361 mg/100g due to soy sauce seasoning, which are clinically relevant for cardiovascular and hypertension management. The dish is not suitable for halal diets due to pork content. It is commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed rice or congee in Malaysian and Singaporean hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and home kitchens. The dish is culturally associated with Chinese-Malaysian and Chinese-Singaporean home cooking traditions and is a staple comfort food across both countries.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite Malaysian/Singaporean dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central using a weighted composite of fried whole egg and cooked ground pork, adjusted for soy sauce sodium contribution. Input values from the GrabFood-sourced record are consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach rather than a direct approved-source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite Malaysian fried egg with minced pork dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16; individual component data exists but reliable composite values cannot be derived without validated recipe proportions. Fried egg with minced pork; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (1.67 g/100g); glycemic_index is set to 0 as a numeric sentinel value per template requirement, gi_category set to 'not_applicable'. The input record contained erroneous ethnic_tags ('Bak Kut Teh', 'Soup') which have been corrected to reflect the actual dish identity. The dish is not halal due to pork content. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as supporting 'menu_listing' only and does not constitute an approved nutrient source; nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central composite estimate. Original input confidence of 0.7 (ai_generated, grabfood provenance) has been superseded by medium confidence based on USDA composite estimation. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 to comply with allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] per G5 hard gate requirement.
